topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me like a physical blow. I stood at the edge of the clearing, my breath catching in my throat, staring at the raw stump where the towering oak used to stand. It was gone. Vanished. My hands were trembling, the soil clinging to my palms, a sickening reminder of what had been. I remembered the sun-drenched afternoons I spent with that sapling, the earnest belief that I was nurturing something permanent. Now, only jagged wood remained, a stark monument to my youthful naiveté. I sank to my knees, the sound of my ragged breathing loud in the sudden silence of the woods. Every instinct screamed at me to run, to flee this scene of utter devastation, but my feet remained rooted, unable to move from the spot where my little tree had once flourished.

[story 2]

I kept running my fingers over the exposed roots, tracing the lines where the trunk had been. A cold knot tightened in my chest, pulling the air from my lungs. It wasn't just a tree; it was a promise I’d made to myself as a child, a small, green commitment made under a sky that seemed infinite. The realization hit me with brutal clarity—someone had taken it. I scrubbed my face with the back of my hand, feeling the heat rise in my cheeks. I wanted to scream, to demand answers from the empty woods, but only a dry, pathetic sound escaped my lips. I looked up at the canopy above, the familiar green replaced by a harsh, indifferent blue, and a deep sense of foolishness washed over me, heavy and suffocating.

[story 3]

He was standing by the clearing, a heavy-set man with calloused hands, looking at the cleared patch of earth with a professional detachment that felt crueler than anger. When he finally looked up, his eyes met mine, and the casualness evaporated. I felt an immediate, sharp flush spread across my neck. I couldn't speak. My mouth felt cotton-dry. I shuffled backward, tripping over a root, and scrambled to put distance between myself and the scene. The silence stretched, thick and unbearable, punctuated only by the distant hum of traffic. I watched him turn away, his shoulders slumping slightly, and I stood there, paralyzed, feeling utterly exposed, as if every small, hopeful thing I possessed had been brutally stripped away.
